title: Respiratory acidosis
body_html:
Definition
Respiratory acidosis occurs when CO₂ retention leads to an increase in arterial PaCO₂, causing a decrease in blood pH (acidaemia). It results from hypoventilation with inadequate alveolar ventilation to remove CO₂.

Pathophysiology
CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻

↑ CO₂ drives the reaction to the right leading to an increase in H⁺ which lowers pH.

The kidneys respond by retaining bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) to compensate but this takes hours to days.

Causes
  • Acute respiratory acidosis (develops quickly, minimal renal compensation)
  • Airway obstruction (foreign body, severe asthma, COPD exacerbation)
  • Respiratory depression (drugs such as benzodiazepines and opiates, head injury)
  • Neuromuscular weakness (Guillain–Barré, myasthenia gravis)
  • Chest wall restriction (severe kyphoscoliosis)
  • Chronic respiratory acidosis (gradual onset, renal compensation present)
  • COPD
  • Obesity hypoventilation syndrome
  • Slowly progressive neuromuscular disorders

ABG Findings
ParameterAcuteChronic
pH↓Near-normal (partially compensated)
PaCO₂↑↑↑
HCO₃⁻Normal (initially)↑ (renal compensation)

High CO₂, low pH = Respiratory acidosis.

Symptoms
  • Headache, confusion, drowsiness (Increased CO₂ leads to cerebral vasodilation)
  • Dyspnoea
  • Tremor, flapping tremor (CO₂ narcosis in severe cases)
  • Tachycardia, arrhythmia (due to acidaemia)

Management
  • Treat the underlying cause (clear airway, reverse sedation, treat COPD exacerbation).
Support ventilation:
  • Oxygen therapy (carefully in chronic CO₂ retainers)
  • Non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) if hypercapnia persists
  • Intubation and mechanical ventilation if severe or worsening.

Summary
TypeMechanismCompensationExamples
AcuteSudden CO₂ riseMinimal renalAsthma attack, opioid overdose
ChronicSustained CO₂ retentionRenal HCO₃⁻ retentionCOPD, obesity hypoventilation
